title: Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil

seo.title: Focaccia Genovese: Authentic Rosemary Olive Oil Recipe
seo.metaDescription: Master authentic Focaccia Genovese with our easy recipe! Learn to make this classic Italian bread with rosemary, olive oil, and a perfect chewy crust. Get baking!
seo.h1: Authentic Focaccia Genovese with Rosemary & Olive Oil
h2Headings: Step-by-Step: How to Make Focaccia Genovese
Tips for the Best Focaccia Genovese
seo.imageAlt: A close-up of freshly baked authentic Focaccia Genovese with rosemary and olive oil, golden brown and dimpled.
seo.imageCaption: Enjoy the irresistible aroma and taste of homemade Focaccia Genovese, a true Italian classic perfect for any meal.

description: Experience the true taste of Liguria with this authentic Focaccia Genovese, a beloved Italian flatbread. Its golden, crisp crust gives way to a soft, airy interior, infused with fragrant rosemary and rich olive oil. Perfect as an appetizer, side, or light meal, this focaccia brings the essence of Genoa right to your kitchen.

ingredients.notes: for dough, pan, and topping, about 1/4 cup + more for drizzling

instructions.title: Activate the Yeast
instructions.text: In a large mixing bowl, combine the warm water and active dry yeast. Stir gently and let it sit for 5-10 minutes until a foamy layer appears on the surface, indicating the yeast is active and ready.

instructions.title: Mix the Dough
instructions.text: Add the all-purpose flour and fine sea salt to the activated yeast mixture. Mix with a sturdy spoon or your hands until a shaggy, wet dough forms. Drizzle in 2 tablespoons of the extra virgin olive oil and continue mixing until just incorporated. The dough will be very sticky and soft.

instructions.title: First Rise and Stretch
instructions.text: Cover the bowl with plastic wrap or a damp kitchen towel. Let the dough rise at room temperature for 1-2 hours, or until it has visibly doubled in size. During this time, perform 2-3 sets of 'stretch and folds' every 30 minutes: wet your hands, gently pull a section of the dough up from the edge, and fold it over itself towards the center, rotating the bowl as you go.

instructions.title: Prepare for Baking
instructions.text: Generously drizzle about 2 tablespoons of extra virgin olive oil into a 9x13 inch (23x33 cm) baking pan, ensuring the bottom and sides are well coated. Gently transfer the risen dough into the oiled pan, turning it once to coat both sides with oil. Carefully spread the dough to fill the pan, being gentle to avoid deflating it too much.

instructions.title: Second Rise and Dimpling
instructions.text: Cover the pan loosely with plastic wrap or a damp cloth and let the dough rise for another 30-45 minutes. Meanwhile, preheat your oven to 220C (425F). Once risen, use your oiled fingertips to gently create deep dimples all over the surface of the focaccia. Drizzle generously with another 2-3 tablespoons of olive oil, then sprinkle with the fresh rosemary leaves and flaky sea salt.

instructions.title: Bake the Focaccia
instructions.text: Place the focaccia in the preheated oven and bake for 25-35 minutes, or until it is beautifully golden brown on top and cooked through. The crust should be crisp, and the interior should feel soft and airy.

instructions.title: Cool and Serve
instructions.text: Remove the focaccia from the oven and carefully transfer it to a wire rack to cool slightly. For an extra touch of flavor, you may drizzle with a final touch of fresh olive oil while warm. Serve the Focaccia Genovese warm or at room temperature, cut into squares or strips.

tips: For the characteristic airy texture, ensure your dough is very wet and sticky. Resist the urge to add too much extra flour during mixing, as hydration is key.
Generously dimple the dough with oiled fingertips just before baking; this creates those signature pockets for the olive oil and helps achieve the perfect texture.
Serve your Focaccia Genovese warm, perhaps with a simple antipasto platter, alongside a hearty soup, or as a delicious base for gourmet sandwiches.
article:
Focaccia, the beloved flatbread of Italy, holds a special place in the hearts of bakers and food enthusiasts worldwide. But among its many delightful variations, Focaccia Genovese stands out as a true masterpiece of Italian baking. Hailing from the picturesque Ligurian capital of Genoa, this particular focaccia is celebrated for its incredibly light, airy texture, crisp golden crust, and a generous anointing of high-quality extra virgin olive oil, fragrant rosemary, and coarse sea salt. It’s more than just bread; it's a sensory experience that transports you straight to the sun-drenched Italian coast with every bite.

This article delves into the authentic charm of Focaccia Genovese, exploring its history, the essential ingredients that give it its distinctive character, and a step-by-step guide to mastering this iconic Italian bake in your own kitchen. Prepare to embark on a culinary journey that celebrates simplicity, quality ingredients, and the enduring magic of traditional Italian baking.

## What is Focaccia Genovese?
Focaccia Genovese, or *Fügassa* in the Ligurian dialect, is not just any focaccia. It is the original, the benchmark against which all other focaccias are often measured. What sets Focaccia Genovese apart is its unique combination of characteristics: a relatively thin dough compared to other regional focaccias, an incredibly open and airy crumb, and a signature dimpled surface that acts as tiny reservoirs for the flavorful olive oil and brine mixture. The crust is wonderfully crisp, almost cracker-like in places, contrasting beautifully with the soft, chewy interior.

Historically, this flatbread has been a staple in Genoa for centuries, often enjoyed for breakfast, dipped in coffee or cappuccino, or as a snack throughout the day. Its simplicity is its strength, relying heavily on the quality of its core ingredients—especially the olive oil—to deliver a profound depth of flavor. Unlike some richer breads, Focaccia Genovese maintains a delicate balance, allowing the pure taste of wheat, yeast, and the glorious olive oil to shine through, perfectly complemented by the aromatic rosemary and the satisfying crunch of coarse salt.

## Ingredients for Authentic Focaccia Genovese
Crafting truly authentic Focaccia Genovese requires attention to detail and, crucially, high-quality ingredients. While the list is short, each component plays a vital role in achieving that quintessential Ligurian taste and texture.

*   **High-Quality "00" Flour or Bread Flour:** Italian "00" flour is ideal for its fine texture and lower protein content, resulting in a tender crumb. However, a good quality unbleached bread flour with a protein content around 11-13% will also yield excellent results, providing the necessary gluten development for the focaccia's characteristic chewiness.
*   **Fresh Yeast (or Active Dry Yeast):** Fresh yeast provides a subtle, nuanced flavor and excellent rise. If using active dry yeast, ensure it's fresh and activate it properly. Instant yeast can also be used, often mixed directly with the flour.
*   **Water:** Lukewarm water (around 95-105°F / 35-40°C) is essential for activating the yeast and hydrating the dough.
*   **Extra Virgin Olive Oil (EVOO):** This is non-negotiable. The soul of Focaccia Genovese resides in its olive oil. Use a high-quality, fruity, peppery extra virgin olive oil. Don't be shy with it; it contributes significantly to the flavor, texture, and crispness of the crust.
*   **Fine Sea Salt:** Incorporated into the dough for flavor.
*   **Coarse Sea Salt (or Flaky Sea Salt):** Crucial for sprinkling on top before baking. It provides bursts of salinity and a delightful textural crunch.
*   **Fresh Rosemary:** The classic aromatic herb for Focaccia Genovese. Its piney, slightly peppery notes perfectly complement the olive oil. Use fresh sprigs for the best flavor.
*   **Water-Olive Oil Brine:** A unique characteristic of Genovese focaccia, this mixture (typically water, olive oil, and a pinch of salt) is drizzled over the dimpled dough before the final rise and baking. It creates those iconic tiny pockets of flavor and contributes to the focaccia's signature crispness.

## Step-by-Step: How to Make Focaccia Genovese
Making Focaccia Genovese is a rewarding process that requires patience but is surprisingly simple.

1.  **Activate the Yeast:** In a large bowl, combine lukewarm water with a pinch of sugar (optional, helps activate yeast) and the fresh or active dry yeast. Let it sit for 5-10 minutes until foamy, indicating the yeast is active.
2.  **Mix the Dough:** Add the flour and fine sea salt to the yeast mixture. Begin to mix with a wooden spoon or a stand mixer with a dough hook until a shaggy dough forms. Gradually add about half of the extra virgin olive oil, mixing until fully incorporated.
3.  **Knead the Dough:** Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured surface and knead for 8-10 minutes, or use a stand mixer on medium-low speed for 6-8 minutes, until the dough is smooth, elastic, and passes the windowpane test (you can stretch a small piece thin enough to see light through it without tearing). The dough will be relatively wet and sticky—resist the urge to add too much extra flour.
4.  **First Rise (Bulk Fermentation):** Lightly grease a clean bowl with olive oil. Place the dough in the bowl, turn it once to coat, and cover with plastic wrap or a damp cloth. Let it rise in a warm place for 1.5-2 hours, or until doubled in size.
5.  **Prepare the Pan:** Generously grease a rectangular baking sheet (approx. 13x18 inches or similar) with plenty of extra virgin olive oil.
6.  **Shape and Dimple:** Gently turn the risen dough onto the prepared baking sheet. Without deflating it too much, gently stretch and press the dough to roughly fill the pan. You might need to let it rest for 10-15 minutes if it springs back, then continue stretching. Once spread, generously drizzle more olive oil over the surface. Now, using your fingertips, firmly press into the dough to create deep dimples, ensuring they go all the way down to the pan.
7.  **Second Rise (Proofing):** In a small bowl, whisk together the water-olive oil brine (water, a tablespoon of olive oil, and a pinch of salt). Pour this mixture evenly over the dimpled focaccia. Scatter fresh rosemary sprigs over the top and sprinkle generously with coarse sea salt. Cover loosely with plastic wrap and let it rise again for 30-45 minutes in a warm place, or until visibly puffy.
8.  **Bake:** Preheat your oven to a high temperature, typically 425-450°F (220-230°C). Bake for 20-30 minutes, or until the Focaccia Genovese is deeply golden brown on top and bottom, and sounds hollow when tapped.
9.  **Cool and Serve:** Once baked, immediately transfer the focaccia to a wire rack to cool slightly. Drizzle with a final touch of fresh extra virgin olive oil if desired, then slice and serve warm.

## Tips for the Best Focaccia Genovese
Achieving that perfect Focaccia Genovese experience comes down to a few key techniques and considerations:

*   **Quality Olive Oil is Paramount:** We can't stress this enough. Invest in a good quality extra virgin olive oil. It's not just a cooking fat; it's a primary flavor component. Don't be afraid to use it generously at every stage.
*   **Hydration is Key:** Focaccia dough is a high-hydration dough, meaning it's quite wet and sticky. This high water content is what contributes to its characteristic open, airy crumb. Resist the urge to add too much flour during kneading.
*   **Don't Over-Knead, But Knead Enough:** While you want good gluten development, over-kneading can lead to a tough focaccia. Aim for a smooth, elastic dough that stretches without tearing.
*   **Embrace the Dimples:** The dimpling process isn't just for aesthetics. It creates those wonderful pockets that trap the olive oil and brine, leading to a moist interior and a crispy, flavorful top crust. Don't be shy; press firmly.
*   **Hot Oven, Quick Bake:** A hot oven is crucial for achieving a rapid rise and a beautifully crisp crust without drying out the interior.
*   **Generous Salt:** The coarse sea salt on top provides a vital counterpoint to the richness of the olive oil and the sweetness of the bread. Don't skimp!
*   **Listen to Your Dough:** Pay attention to how the dough feels and looks. Adjust rising times based on the temperature of your kitchen.

## Serving Suggestions for Focaccia Genovese
Focaccia Genovese is incredibly versatile and can be enjoyed in countless ways:

*   **As a Snack or Appetizer:** Simply slice and enjoy on its own, perhaps with a little extra drizzle of olive oil for dipping.
*   **With Italian Meats and Cheeses:** It's the perfect accompaniment to a platter of prosciutto, salami, mortadella, and various Italian cheeses.
*   **Alongside Soups and Salads:** Use it to sop up delicious broths or as a textural contrast to a fresh green salad.
*   **Sandwich Bread:** Its sturdy yet airy structure makes it an excellent choice for sandwiches. Fill it with anything from roasted vegetables to cured meats.
*   **The Traditional Genoese Breakfast:** Yes, seriously! Locals often enjoy Focaccia Genovese dipped in their morning coffee or cappuccino. Don't knock it until you've tried it!
*   **Dipped in Balsamic Vinegar:** A classic pairing, though the true Ligurian way emphasizes the focaccia's inherent flavor.

## Variations & Substitutions for Focaccia
While the classic Focaccia Genovese with rosemary and olive oil is perfection in itself, focaccia is a versatile canvas. However, it's important to note that any significant deviation from the core recipe would technically make it a *different* type of focaccia, not authentic Focaccia Genovese.

*   **Other Herbs:** While rosemary is traditional for Genovese, other focaccias might feature thyme, oregano, or a mix of Italian herbs.
*   **Toppings:** Beyond rosemary and salt, common focaccia toppings include thinly sliced onions, cherry tomatoes (often pressed into the dimples), olives, or even thinly sliced potatoes. These are delightful but transform it into a different regional style.
*   **Cheese:** Some variations incorporate cheese, either mixed into the dough or sprinkled on top during the last few minutes of baking.
*   **Gluten-Free Focaccia:** While possible with specialized flour blends, achieving the exact texture and crumb of traditional wheat-based focaccia is challenging. It would be a gluten-free focaccia, not a classic Focaccia Genovese.

The beauty of Focaccia Genovese lies in its elegant simplicity and the exquisite harmony of a few humble ingredients. By following these guidelines and embracing the process, you can bring a piece of Ligurian culinary tradition into your home. The aroma of baking rosemary and olive oil will fill your kitchen, promising a taste of Italy that is both authentic and utterly irresistible. Enjoy the journey of mastering this iconic Italian flatbread.
